editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: India spends only about 0.64% of GDP on R&D, far below its 2% target and global leaders, yet paradoxically ranks third in scientific papers and sixth in patents, showcasing both critical underinvestment and remarkable efficiency that could be leveraged if spending were increased.
requiredConcepts: The framing fact to open on: India spends about 0.64% of its GDP on research and development (DST, 2020-21), and the ratio has been stuck in the 0.6-0.7% band for about two decades. It is far below the leaders (Israel about 6.3%, South Korea about 4.9% in 2023), below the United States, Japan and Germany (all above 3%), below China (about 2.6%) and below the world average (about 2.6%). State this as the gap the whole article sits inside

The honest nuance on the trend: India's R&D intensity actually nudged UP toward about 0.8% of GDP around 2008-2011 and then slid back to about 0.64%, so the truthful description is 'peaked over a decade ago and drifted down', not simply 'flat'. The World Bank/UNESCO series is the continuous India series used for the trend; DST corroborates it

The rupee paradox: in current rupees India's gross expenditure on R&D more than doubled, from about ₹60,000 crore in 2010-11 to about ₹1.27 lakh crore in 2020-21. Yet as a share of GDP it barely moved, because the economy grew just as fast, and part of even the rupee rise is inflation. Both facts are true at once and the article must hold them together

The 2% target: India's 2013 Science, Technology and Innovation Policy set a goal of 2% of GDP, and NITI Aayog restated it in 2026 (raise from 0.64% to at least 2%). It has gone unmet for over a decade with no credible path visible. The 2% line on the chart is a policy target, not a measurement

Scale in absolute terms: measured in purchasing-power dollars India's R&D was about 58 billion PPP-dollars in 2020-21, against roughly 760 billion for the United States and about 860 billion for China (OECD, 2024). India is about a fifteenth of China's R&D spend in absolute money, a far larger gap than the GDP-share comparison alone suggests. India was about 3.1% of world R&D in 2018 (UNESCO). Flag that the China/US figures are 2024 and India's is 2020-21

Who PERFORMS India's R&D (DST, 2020-21): central government about 43.7%, private-sector industry about 36.4%, higher education about 8.8%, state governments about 6.7%, public-sector industry about 4.4%. The public bloc (central plus state plus universities) is nearly 60%. This is the reverse of advanced economies

The business-sector gap, the single clearest structural diagnosis: in South Korea, China and the United States businesses fund or perform roughly three-quarters or more of all R&D (about 79%, 77%, 75%; Germany about 67%), while in India the private sector is only about 36%. Be honest that India is absent from the OECD's comparable database, so its figure is DST's own survey and is the performer share, roughly but not exactly comparable in definition

Where India's public research money goes (DST, 2020-21, shares of central-government R&D): DRDO/defence about 30.7%, Department of Space about 18.4%, ICAR/agriculture about 12.4%, Department of Atomic Energy about 11.4%, CSIR about 8.2%. Defence, space and atomic energy together take about 60%. Twelve major agencies account for about 84% of central-government R&D. A strategic tilt is a deliberate choice, not an accident, but it leaves civilian, university and health research thin

The people shortage: India has only about 260 researchers per million people (DST 262 in 2020; the World Bank's 259 agrees), against roughly 9,500 in South Korea, about 5,900 in Germany, about 5,600 in Japan, about 4,900 in the United States, about 2,100 in China and a world average near 1,650. Strikingly, India sits below Vietnam (about 836) and South Africa (about 444). India's own series rose from about 110 (2000) to 218 (2015), 255 (2017) and 262 (2020), real progress from a very low base

The China-and-Korea divergence: two generations ago India and China spent similar small shares on research; China then climbed from under 1% to about 2.6% of GDP and Korea to nearly 5%, while India's line stayed near 0.6%. Because these are shares of very differently sized economies, China's lead in absolute dollars is larger still

The efficiency paradox, the spine of the middle of the article, stated carefully: India spends like an also-ran but produces like a heavyweight. It published about 278,000 scientific papers in 2022, third in the world behind only China (about a million) and the United States (about 721,000), having overtaken the United Kingdom, Germany and Japan (NSF/Scopus). On the WIPO Global Innovation Index 2025 it ranks 38th overall but 32nd on innovation outputs against 52nd on inputs, and has been an 'overperformer' for fifteen years running

The honest caveats on the paradox, which must be stated so the article does not over-claim: publication counts measure VOLUME, not citations or impact, where India ranks lower; today's output partly reflects research capacity and people trained years ago, not current spending; and 'punching above your weight' is not the same as spending enough. Efficiency is a reason for optimism about what more money could buy, not evidence that the spending gap does not matter

Patents, the genuine bright spot: in filings AT the Indian Patent Office, domestic (resident) applicants drew level with foreign applicants in 2022 for the first time and reached about 60% of filings by 2024; India is now sixth in the world for total patent filings (WIPO). Note these are calendar-year, office-level filing counts; filings are an input/intermediate indicator, not commercialised inventions

Why research pays, the case for spending more, stated as established economics: the measured social return to R&D is high, commonly put around 30-50% a year, and runs well above the private return a single firm captures (often put around 7-30%), with influential work (Bloom, Van Reenen and co-authors) estimating social returns roughly four times private. Agricultural research has some of the highest measured returns of all (often 40-60% a year; the international CGIAR system is credited with benefit-cost ratios around 10 to 1). These are midpoints of wide, contested ranges, not precise figures; the gap between social and private return is the textbook reason governments fund research at all

The public-seedbed argument, as context: a large body of work (Mariana Mazzucato's 'Entrepreneurial State'; Vannevar Bush's 1945 'Science, the Endless Frontier'; the DARPA model) holds that publicly funded basic research seeded technologies private firms later commercialised, from the internet and GPS to the touchscreen. Present it as an influential argument with critics (who say it overstates the state's role and understates the private adaptation required), not as settled fact

Good uses, India's frugal-science success, with figures: India reached Mars (Mangalyaan) for about 74 million dollars and landed near the Moon's south pole (Chandrayaan-3) for about 75 million, against roughly 580 million for NASA's MAVEN Mars orbiter and about 133 million for Russia's failed Luna-25. The ISRO model is mission-focused, autonomous and frugal. These are headline mission costs that exclude shared overheads and decades of prior investment, so the point is the order-of-magnitude frugality, not an exact ratio. India's vaccine-manufacturing base (Serum Institute, Bharat Biotech with ICMR on Covaxin) and CSIR's generic-drug process chemistry are other genuine public/public-private wins

Bad uses and waste, told fairly: money that never gets spent (in 2023-24 the science department used only about a third of its budgeted R&D head and the new research foundation barely an eighth; DST under-spent its budget by roughly 18% a year on average over 2018-24), driven by government financial rules, automatic year-end withdrawal of unspent funds, GST on research equipment and procurement delays (NITI Aayog, 2026). The 'valley of death' between lab and market, where a large majority of patents are never commercialised. Long-running prestige and defence projects with deep overruns, the clearest being the Kaveri jet engine, in development for about 39 years and still not powering a fighter, which is why India bought foreign engines. State these as documented problems, not as proof the whole system is wasteful

Why India under-invests, the structural diagnosis, presented as several contributing factors and never a single cause (NITI Aayog 2025-26 and others): a weak private and philanthropic research base; an economy whose large IT-services sector does relatively little frontier R&D; brain drain (tens of thousands of Indian-origin researchers abroad); thin university research; fragmented funding across many agencies; and concentration of what funding exists (consultation input suggests the IIT system receives a very large share of the new foundation's grants, with grant-application success rates reported under 10%). India has on the order of 1,800 public R&D institutes

The new bets, stated with their catch: the Anusandhan National Research Foundation (ANRF, created 2023) is built on a target of ₹50,000 crore over 2023-28, of which only ₹14,000 crore is committed government money and ₹36,000 crore is meant to come from private and other sources, an aspiration rather than committed funding; a separate Research, Development and Innovation (RDI) fund adds a ₹1 lakh crore financing corpus aimed at private, higher-readiness projects, which lends or takes equity rather than giving grants. Whether private money actually turns up is the open question on which the 2% target depends

VINTAGE DISCIPLINE is absolute: India's official R&D statistics (rupee spend, sector and agency splits, researcher density) come from DST/NSTMIS and end at 2020-21; always make the year explicit and never imply these are current-year figures. The cross-country World Bank/UNESCO numbers are mostly 2020-2023. The NITI Aayog 2026 reports are fresh but largely restate the same 2020-21 base data

NUMBER DISCIPLINE is absolute: every statistic, share, rupee or dollar figure in the prose must come from the evidence packet's locked numbers or the specific figures stated in these concepts; never invent, recall or estimate a number. Present contested or derived figures (returns to R&D, absolute PPP comparisons, the private-sector share) as approximate ranges, never false-precision decimals. Anchor every number when it first appears (what it measures, against what, high or low in plain words)

Keep the macha layer warm and plain-spoken, a sharp friend explaining what India's research gap really means for the country's future, with the playfulness in the headings and the body grounded and genuinely helpful; it summarises what the whole page means, not one chart

Clarify that this is not about total money but about the share of the economy devoted to research.

sectionPlan.caveat: China's economy is also much larger, so absolute R&D spending diverged even more.

sectionPlan.heading: How many researchers does India have?
sectionPlan.job: Compare researchers per million: South Korea ~9,472, Germany ~5,926, Japan ~5,609, US ~4,937, China ~2,107, World ~1,651, Vietnam ~836, South Africa ~444, India ~259. India is far below Vietnam and South Africa.
lockedNumbersToUse: South Korea — Researchers in R&D, latest (9,472)
sectionPlan.caveat: India's latest figure is 2020; others are 2022-2023. Full-time equivalent definitions may differ.

sectionPlan.heading: Is India's researcher base growing?
sectionPlan.job: Show India's own DST series: 110 (2000) → 218 (2015) → 255 (2017) → 262 (2020) researchers per million. Real progress from a very low base, but the climb is slow.
sectionPlan.caveat: Growth is real but the absolute number is still tiny by global standards.

sectionPlan.heading: What does the spending gap look like in absolute money terms?
sectionPlan.job: Contrast absolute R&D spend in PPP dollars: China $860B (2024), US $760B (2024), India $58B (2020-21). India is about 1/15th of China and 1/13th of the US. The GDP-share comparison hides the vast scale difference.
sectionPlan.caveat: India's data is 2020-21; China and US are 2024; India's share likely didn't change much but the absolute gap may have widened.

sectionPlan.heading: But doesn't India produce a lot of research anyway?
sectionPlan.job: Highlight that India ranked 3rd in scientific publications in 2022: China ~1,000,000, US ~721,000, India ~278,000. Note that volume is not impact, and today's output reflects past capacity.
lockedNumbersToUse: Third in research papers: China 1,000 thousand
Third in research papers: United States 721 thousand
Third in research papers: India 278 thousand
sectionPlan.caveat: Counts measure quantity, not quality or influence; India's citation rankings are lower.

sectionPlan.heading: What about patents?
sectionPlan.job: Show resident (Indian) patent filings crossing non-resident (foreign) filings in 2022 and reaching ~60% share by 2024. India is now 6th in total filings worldwide. Filings are an input, not commercialised inventions.
sectionPlan.caveat: Patent office data; a surge in filings may be driven by fee changes or awareness, not necessarily innovation quality.

sectionPlan.heading: Is India more efficient at turning money into innovation?
sectionPlan.job: Show India's GII 2025 ranks: innovation inputs 52nd, outputs 32nd. Consistently an 'overperformer' for 15 years. Efficiency is a reason for optimism about what more money could buy.
lockedNumbersToUse: Spends like 50th, innovates like 30th: inputs rank 52
Spends like 50th, innovates like 30th: outputs rank 32
sectionPlan.caveat: GII is a composite index with many subjective components; overperformance doesn't mean the spending gap doesn't matter.

sectionPlan.heading: What are India's best examples of research on a budget?
sectionPlan.job: Cite frugal space missions: Mangalyaan ($74M), Chandrayaan-3 ($75M) vs NASA MAVEN ($582M) and Russia Luna-25 ($133M). Mention ISRO's model, vaccine manufacturing, CSIR's generic-drug chemistry.
sectionPlan.caveat: Headline mission costs exclude shared overheads and decades of prior investment; the order-of-magnitude difference is the point.

sectionPlan.heading: What goes wrong with India's research spending?
sectionPlan.job: Show budget utilisation: DST average 82% (2018-24), but its R&D head only 34% in 2023-24, ANRF a mere 13%. Explain procedural hurdles, the 'valley of death', and prestige projects like the Kaveri engine (39 years, not in service).
sectionPlan.caveat: Underutilisation is not the whole story; some unspent funds may be due to multi-year projects, but the pattern is chronic.

sectionPlan.heading: What is India doing to fix the gap?
sectionPlan.job: Introduce ANRF (₹50,000 crore over 5 years, only ₹14,000 crore committed by government) and RDI fund (₹1 lakh crore corpus for loans/equity). The 2% target depends on private money showing up.
conceptsToExplain: RDI fund as financing, not grant
sectionPlan.caveat: The private share is aspirational; if it doesn't materialise, the 2% target remains out of reach.

sectionPlan.heading: How should you read these numbers?
sectionPlan.job: Source transparency and methodology. Name key sources (World Bank, UNESCO, DST/NSTMIS, NITI Aayog, WIPO, NSF, OECD). Flag data lags (Indian official data ends at 2020-21), comparability issues (India not in OECD's business R&D database), and that returns figures are midpoints of contested ranges. Emphasise that multiple factors move together, not one cause.
sectionPlan.caveat: These numbers are the best available, but they come with long lags and conceptual mismatches; treat the big picture as robust, the precise figures as approximate.

chartPlan.title: The world spends very differently on research
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The single number that frames the whole article: India spends about 0.6% of its economy on research, a fraction of what the leaders spend.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each bar shows R&D spending as a percentage of GDP for a country in its latest available year. The longer the bar, the larger the share of the economy devoted to research.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Do not read this as total rupees or dollars spent. It is a share of GDP, so a smaller but research-intensive economy can have a higher bar.

chartPlan.title: Research pays: the question is who reaps it
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Before asking whether India spends enough, it helps to know what research is worth: the measured returns are high.
chartPlan.howToRead: The bars represent midpoint estimates of annual returns from academic literature. Social returns (the benefit to the whole economy) greatly exceed private returns (what a firm captures).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are not observed numbers but estimates from studies with wide ranges. Do not treat them as guaranteed returns for any specific project.

chartPlan.title: India's research spending peaked over a decade ago
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India set itself a 2% target in 2013 and has drifted further from it, not closer.
chartPlan.howToRead: The solid lines show India's and the world's R&D intensity over time. The dashed line is the 2% target set in India's 2013 policy. India's line briefly rose, then fell back.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse the target line with actual spending; it's a goal, not a measurement. Also, the world average includes India, so the gap is even wider for the rest of the world.

chartPlan.title: More rupees, the same thin sliver
chartPlan.whyShowThis: In rupees, India's research budget keeps climbing; the catch is that the economy grew just as fast.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line charts India's gross R&D expenditure in current rupees. The upward slope shows rising nominal spending, but when divided by a fast-growing GDP, the share stays flat.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't mistake the rupee rise for an increase in real research effort; part is inflation, and part is GDP growth. The share matters more for comparison.

chartPlan.title: In India, the government still does the research
chartPlan.whyShowThis: In most advanced economies business does most of the research; in India the state still dominates.
chartPlan.howToRead: Each bar shows the share of national R&D performed by a sector. The central government and private industry are the two biggest performers, but the public sector overall (central, state, universities, public industry) accounts for nearly 60%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Performer share is not the same as funder share. Some government-performed research may be funded by industry, and vice versa.

chartPlan.title: Everywhere else, business pays for research
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The clearest single diagnosis of India's R&D gap: its private sector barely shows up.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars represent the share of total R&D funded or performed by business. For most comparator countries it is 67-79%, for India it is 36%. India's figure is a performer share from its own survey.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: India's figure is not directly comparable to the OECD's 'business enterprise R&D' definition. It is a rough order-of-magnitude comparison, not a precise match.

chartPlan.title: Where India's public research money goes
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The public money that does exist is tilted heavily toward defence, space and atomic energy.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show the percentage of central-government R&D expenditure by agency. Defence, space and atomic energy together consume about 60%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is only central-government R&D, not all public R&D. State governments and universities have different priorities, but the central tilt is stark.

chartPlan.title: China and Korea pulled away
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Two generations ago India and China spent similar shares on research; then their paths split.
chartPlan.howToRead: The multi-line chart tracks R&D intensity for India, China, South Korea and the world since the mid-1990s. China and Korea climb steeply, while India and the world average diverge slowly.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume India started at the same level as Korea. Korea was already higher in 1996, but the trajectories illustrate vastly different ambitions.

chartPlan.title: India has strikingly few researchers
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Money is only half of it; the other half is people, and India has remarkably few researchers for its size.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show researchers per million population. India's bar is far shorter than all comparators except perhaps some fragile states. Note the logarithmic scale mentally—South Korea has over 9,000 per million, India under 260.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is full-time equivalent researchers; definitions can vary. Also, many Indian researchers may be abroad, so the stock of Indian-origin researchers is higher than the domestic count.

chartPlan.title: India is adding researchers, but from a very low base
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The trend is up, which is real, but the starting point is so low that the climb barely registers globally.
chartPlan.howToRead: The line shows India's own estimate of researchers per million over select years. It has more than doubled since 2000, but remains under 300.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The absolute increase is significant (from 110 to 262), but relative to a population of 1.4 billion, the stock is tiny. Growth rates can be high on small bases.

chartPlan.title: The absolute scale of the gap
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shares of GDP understate the gap; in absolute money the distance is an order of magnitude.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars compare R&D spending in purchasing-power-parity dollars. China's bar is about 15 times India's, even though its GDP share is about 4 times India's.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: PPP dollars adjust for price levels, but the figures are from different years (India 2020-21, others 2024), so the gap is even larger in reality.

chartPlan.title: And yet: third in the world for research papers
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Here is the paradox: India spends like an also-ran but produces like a heavyweight.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show thousands of scientific papers published in 2022. India is third, behind China and the US, but ahead of the UK, Germany, and Japan.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is quantity, not quality. Citation-based metrics often rank India lower. Also, India's publication count is boosted by a large academic workforce, not necessarily by cutting-edge R&D.

chartPlan.title: Indians now file most of their own patents
chartPlan.whyShowThis: A genuine shift: domestic inventors have gone from a minority of patent filings to a clear majority.
chartPlan.howToRead: The two lines show patent applications at the Indian office by resident (Indian) and non-resident (foreign) applicants. Resident filings crossed non-resident in 2022 and now dominate.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Filing is not granting, and granting is not commercialisation. This is an input measure of inventive activity, not an output measure of innovation.

chartPlan.title: India spends like 50th, innovates like 30th
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The paradox in one chart: India converts modest inputs into outsized innovation output.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show India's rank out of 139 economies on innovation inputs (52) and outputs (32). Lower rank is better. The gap shows overperformance.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The GII is a composite index with many components; overperformance here does not mean every research rupee is optimally spent. It suggests latent capacity.

chartPlan.title: India does science cheap: the ISRO model
chartPlan.whyShowThis: The best of Indian research is mission-focused and frugal, doing for tens of millions what others spend hundreds on.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars compare the headline cost of India's Mars and Moon missions with comparable foreign missions. India's missions cost roughly an order of magnitude less.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: These are mission-specific budgets, not total program costs. They exclude shared infrastructure and prior R&D, but the frugality is real.

chartPlan.title: The research money that doesn't get spent
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Some of India's R&D problem is not too little money but money that never gets out the door.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show the share of budgeted allocation actually spent. For DST's R&D head, only 34% was used in 2023-24; for ANRF, only 13%.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Underutilisation can also reflect multi-year projects or late releases, but chronic low utilisation across multiple agencies suggests systemic bottlenecks.

chartPlan.title: The new bets on Indian research
chartPlan.whyShowThis: India's answer is a set of large new vehicles, betting that private money will finally show up.
chartPlan.howToRead: Bars show the headline commitments: ANRF's government share (₹14,000 crore) and private target (₹36,000 crore), and the RDI Fund's corpus (₹1 lakh crore). The private target is aspirational.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Don't treat the ₹36,000 crore or ₹1 lakh crore as committed money. The RDI fund is a financing vehicle, not a grant programme. Realisation depends on private uptake.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Bar chart allows immediate comparison across countries; latest point is most relevant for policy debate.
Simple bar chart of estimated returns; suitable for a conceptual point rather than a time series.
Line chart over time shows the trend and the target gap; world average as reference.
Rising rupee line shows the nominal increase; essential to pair with GDP share chart for context.
Sectoral share breakdown is best shown with bars; a pie chart is less precise for small slices.
Cross-country comparison of business share works well as bars; easy to see India's short bar.
Agency breakdown is a single snapshot; bars rank agencies clearly.
Long-term trajectories of four series tell the divergence story; lines are the most natural.
Bars quickly convey relative researcher density; the disparity is immediate.
The upward trend matters; a line with sparse points shows progress.
Huge disparity in absolute dollars; bars work.
Top-three comparison is simple; bars are fine.
Two lines crossing tells a story; multi-line is ideal.
Two ranks, simple bars.
Bars compare mission costs directly.
Utilisation rates as bars; no time series needed.
Commitments as bars, with a note on private aspiration.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Total domestic spending on research and experimental development, divided by the country's gross domestic product. It measures how much of the economy's output is ploughed back into creating new knowledge and innovations.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: This is the headline metric used throughout the article to compare research effort across countries. A low share means a small slice of national output goes to R&D.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The number of full-time equivalent researchers (scientists and engineers engaged in the conception or creation of new knowledge) per one million population. It shows the density of the research workforce.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's low researcher density reveals a deep talent gap that compounds the spending gap.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The total money spent on research and development in a country, from all sources (government, business, universities, nonprofits). It is the rupee total before adjusting for inflation or economic size.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: We need GERD to show the rupee paradox: the total keeps rising but the share of GDP does not.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: An adjustment that converts local currencies into an international dollar that buys the same basket of goods in each country. It makes absolute spending comparable by stripping out price-level differences.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Without PPP, the absolute gap is distorted by lower costs in India. Even in PPP terms, India's R&D is dwarfed by China and the US.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: An application for patent protection at a national office. Resident filings come from inventors based in the country; non-resident filings come from overseas. It is an early indicator of inventive activity, but not all filings become granted patents or commercialised.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The crossover of resident filings signals growing domestic R&D ambition, even if the step to commercialisation is still missing.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A composite ranking by WIPO that scores countries on innovation inputs (institutions, human capital, infrastructure, market and business sophistication) and outputs (knowledge and technology outputs, creative outputs). Overperformance means outputs rank higher than inputs.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's consistent overperformance is the clearest evidence of R&D efficiency, but it does not justify underinvestment.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: India's policy document that set the goal of raising R&D expenditure to 2% of GDP. It is a declaration of intent, not a binding budget.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The 2% target line on several charts traces this unmet aspiration, framing the failure to close the gap.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: A new research funding body created in 2023 to coordinate and fund R&D across disciplines, with a target corpus of ₹50,000 crore over five years, of which only ₹14,000 crore is committed government money. It is meant to attract large private contributions.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: ANRF is India's main institutional bet to fix the funding gap, but its success hinges on private money that has yet to materialise.

glossaryPlan.term: Social return vs private return to R&D
glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Private return is the profit a firm earns from its own R&D. Social return includes the broader benefits to other firms and society—spillovers from new knowledge. Because firms can't capture all the benefits, markets underinvest in R&D, which is why government funding is theoretically needed.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The large gap between social and private returns is the economic case for why India's underinvestment matters; the benefits to the country far exceed what any single firm would spontaneously fund.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: India's national space agency, known for low-cost, high-impact missions. It exemplifies a mission-driven, frugal approach to public R&D.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: ISRO's cost-effectiveness shows that when research is well-focused, India can achieve world-class results on a shoestring, suggesting that more money could be used well.

proseRules: No calculations unless the evidence packet supplies the derived number
Use only lockedNumbers; never invent or recall figures
Vintage discipline: always specify the year for Indian official stats (2020-21) and for cross-country (various).
Anchor every number: what it measures, against what, high or low in plain words.
No em-dashes; no 'not just X, it is Y' constructions.
Round numbers like a human: 0.6%, not 0.64558; 1.3 lakh crore, not 127381 crore.
Macha layer warm and plain-spoken, a friend explaining what the gap means.
Caveat every derived or contested number; present returns as midpoints of wide ranges.
Never claim a single cause for India's under-investment; list multiple factors.

visualPlan.mobileGuidance: Prefer horizontal bars, share strips, or at most three lines; avoid dense tables.
visualPlan.whyThisMatters: This is the chart that answers the page question most directly.

plannedCharts.title: The world spends very differently on research
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO · gross R&D spending as a share of GDP · latest available year per country
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single number that frames the whole article: India spends about 0.6% of its economy on research, a fraction of what the leaders spend.

plannedCharts.title: Research pays: the question is who reaps it
plannedCharts.subtitle: Economics literature · midpoints of wide ranges, shown to make a point, not as precise measurements
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Before asking whether India spends enough, it helps to know what research is worth: the measured returns are high.

plannedCharts.title: India's research spending peaked over a decade ago
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO (India, World) · against the 2% of GDP target set in the 2013 STI Policy
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India set itself a 2% target in 2013 and has drifted further from it, not closer.

plannedCharts.title: More rupees, the same thin sliver
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · gross expenditure on R&D, current prices · fiscal years, latest 2020-21
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: In rupees, India's research budget keeps climbing; the catch is that the economy grew just as fast.

plannedCharts.title: In India, the government still does the research
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · who performs India's R&D · 2020-21
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: In most advanced economies business does most of the research; in India the state still dominates.

plannedCharts.title: Everywhere else, business pays for research
plannedCharts.unit: % of R&D from business / private sector
plannedCharts.subtitle: OECD / WIPO / NITI Aayog · business-enterprise share of R&D · India is its private-industry share (DST 2020-21)
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The clearest single diagnosis of India's R&D gap: its private sector barely shows up.

plannedCharts.title: Where India's public research money goes
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · central-government R&D by agency · 2020-21
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The public money that does exist is tilted heavily toward defence, space and atomic energy.

plannedCharts.title: China and Korea pulled away
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO · R&D spending as a share of GDP, India vs the risers
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Two generations ago India and China spent similar shares on research; then their paths split.

plannedCharts.title: India has strikingly few researchers
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank / UNESCO · full-time-equivalent researchers per million population · latest available per country
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Money is only half of it; the other half is people, and India has remarkably few researchers for its size.

plannedCharts.title: India is adding researchers, but from a very low base
plannedCharts.subtitle: DST / NSTMIS · researchers per million population in India · selected years
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The trend is up, which is real, but the starting point is so low that the climb barely registers globally.

plannedCharts.title: The absolute scale of the gap
plannedCharts.subtitle: OECD MSTI (China, US · 2024) and DST / UNESCO (India · 2020-21) · purchasing-power-parity dollars
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Shares of GDP understate the gap; in absolute money the distance is an order of magnitude.

plannedCharts.title: And yet: third in the world for research papers
plannedCharts.subtitle: NSF Science & Engineering Indicators / Scopus · scientific & technical publications · 2022
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Here is the paradox: India spends like an also-ran but produces like a heavyweight.

plannedCharts.title: Indians now file most of their own patents
plannedCharts.subtitle: WIPO / World Bank · applications filed at the Indian Patent Office, by applicant
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: A genuine shift: domestic inventors have gone from a minority of patent filings to a clear majority.

plannedCharts.title: India spends like 50th, innovates like 30th
plannedCharts.subtitle: WIPO Global Innovation Index 2025 · India's rank on innovation inputs vs outputs
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The paradox in one chart: India converts modest inputs into outsized innovation output.

plannedCharts.title: India does science cheap: the ISRO model
plannedCharts.subtitle: Mission budgets · India's space programme vs comparable foreign missions · approximate
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The best of Indian research is mission-focused and frugal, doing for tens of millions what others spend hundreds on.

plannedCharts.title: The research money that doesn't get spent
plannedCharts.subtitle: Open Budgets India · share of the budgeted R&D allocation actually used
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Some of India's R&D problem is not too little money but money that never gets out the door.

plannedCharts.title: The new bets on Indian research
plannedCharts.subtitle: ANRF / RDI Scheme · the headline new funding commitments
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's answer is a set of large new vehicles, betting that private money will finally show up.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
